justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Timeout mismatch clearly identified — sequential p99 sum of 1150ms vs 500ms inbound, called out as '2.3x the budget'. (2) Orphaned downstream request consequence thoroughly described — explains upstream abandons but downstream continues, leading to thread/connection pool exhaustion. (3) Outbound > inbound relationship explicitly identified — '6:1 mismatch' with 3×1000ms vs 500ms. (4) Multiple remediation options provided: parallelization, context deadline propagation (~450ms), negotiating higher inbound timeout, making InventoryService async/optional, circuit breakers. (5) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — goes beyond the rubric by noting that even parallelized, InventoryService p99 (800ms) alone exceeds the 500ms budget, which is a deeper insight than just computing the sequential sum.
Correctly identifies the orphaned request problem and its cascade consequences: thread pool exhaustion, connection pool starvation, upstream retry amplification.
Provides more than two remediation options: parallelization, reduced outbound timeouts with shared context deadline, increased inbound timeout, circuit breakers, and async/optional enrichment.
Appropriate escalation to MJ for the architectural decision on upstream contract and InventoryService SLA.
Strong operational detail — covers missing observability (per-service histograms, orphaned request metrics, distributed tracing), graceful degradation gaps, and on-call runbook needs.

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Timeout mismatch clearly identified with explicit arithmetic (200+150+800=1150ms vs 500ms inbound). (2) Orphaned downstream request consequence thoroughly described — wasted work, upstream already disconnected, thread pool exhaustion. (3) Outbound > inbound relationship identified and called 'CRITICALLY MISCONFIGURED' with 6:1 ratio. (4) Multiple remediation options: parallelization, timeout budget reallocation, circuit breakers, degraded partial responses, request deadline propagation. (5) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on the timeout math — not just the sum but per-call analysis showing InventoryService alone exceeds the inbound budget
Correctly identifies that lack of retry logic is actually appropriate given the broken timeout budget — shows nuanced understanding
Health check cascading failure risk is a valuable additional finding beyond the core rubric requirements
Thread/connection pool exhaustion analysis connects the timeout mismatch to a concrete operational failure mode
Concrete timeout values suggested for parallel configuration (400ms per service + 100ms overhead)
Well-calibrated confidence — 60% reflects genuine uncertainty about unspecified upstream behavior and p50 latencies while being certain about the arithmetic

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Timeout mismatch clearly identified with sequential p99 sum of 1150ms vs 500ms inbound timeout, (2) Orphaned downstream request consequence thoroughly described including resource waste and load amplification, (3) Outbound timeout > inbound timeout relationship explicitly called out (3x1000ms vs 500ms), (4) Multiple remediation options provided: parallelization, higher inbound timeout, partial enrichment with deadline budgeting, circuit breakers, context/deadline propagation, (5) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptional depth — goes beyond the rubric by noting that even parallelized, InventoryService p99 (800ms) alone exceeds the 500ms inbound timeout
Identifies that this is not just a tail-latency problem but affects median requests too (p50 sum ~300-500ms)
Correctly identifies the cascade risk: orphaned work, connection pool exhaustion, load amplification on downstream services
Provides four distinct remediation strategies (parallelize, raise inbound timeout, partial enrichment, context propagation) exceeding the two required
Appropriate escalations to MJ for architectural decisions about degradation strategy and upstream contract
Strong observability analysis identifying specific missing metrics (per-service latency histograms, orphaned work counters) needed to diagnose the inevitable production incidents
